title: The toddler who bites his own lips
blurb: An X-linked disorder of purine salvage. Self-mutilation, dystonia, and a uric acid level that doesn't belong in a 2-year-old.

patient.complaint: Self-biting (lips, fingers), worsening 'stiffness', orange crystals in his diapers.

opening: 2-year-old boy referred for evaluation of self-injury and motor regression. He has been biting his lower lip and fingertips, sometimes drawing blood; his parents now mitten his hands. He was a normal infant but developmental milestones have slowed — he's not yet walking. Mother noticed orange-pink gritty residue in his diapers. Uric acid is 13.4. Maternal uncle has gout and intellectual disability. What do you want to ask, examine, or order?
history: Born at term, met early milestones. From ~6 months on, hypotonia evolving into dystonia. From 18 months on, episodes of self-directed biting. Mom states 'he doesn't want to hurt himself — he cries when he does it.' Diet: typical toddler.
exam: Active, dystonic, hyperreflexic. Choreoathetoid movements of the upper extremities. Tongue and lip lacerations (self-inflicted), fingertips with healed bite marks. No dysmorphism. Mild cognitive delay.

prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why does losing a SALVAGE enzyme drive uric acid SO high?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the purine SALVAGE step that fails in this disorder. (It's the cofactor-style arrow that loops hypoxanthine back to IMP.)
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — HGPRT. It normally rescues hypoxanthine back to IMP (and guanine back to GMP) using PRPP. Without HGPRT, PRPP piles up and feedback inhibition on de novo synthesis is released → de novo purine synthesis runs uncontrolled → hyperuricemia.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The block isn't in degradation — it's the loop that BRINGS hypoxanthine back as a usable purine nucleotide.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: HGPRT salvage (Hypoxanthine → IMP). The faded arrow back from hypoxanthine to IMP is the missing recycling step.

mcqs.stem: Why is uric acid SO elevated when only a salvage enzyme is missing?
choices: Loss of HGPRT raises PRPP availability AND drops nucleotide feedback inhibition → de novo synthesis spins, and any hypoxanthine/guanine that isn't salvaged drains to uric acid
HGPRT inhibits xanthine oxidase under normal conditions
Uric acid is the direct product of HGPRT
mcqs.explanation: PRPP that would have been used for salvage builds up and drives de novo synthesis (which itself uses PRPP). Plus, the IMP/GMP feedback on the de novo committed step (PRPP amidotransferase) is relieved. Both effects push the system to crank out purines that ultimately catabolize to uric acid.

mcqs.stem: Allopurinol is started for the hyperuricemia. Which feature of the disorder will it NOT correct?
mcqs.explanation: Allopurinol (and rasburicase) lowers uric acid by blocking xanthine oxidase — it prevents the gout, stones, and nephropathy. The CNS phenotype (self-mutilation, dystonia, intellectual disability) is uric-acid-independent and unfortunately is NOT reversed by uric-acid lowering.

mcqs.stem: Why is the disorder X-linked, with affected males and a maternal uncle's similar history?
choices: It is autosomal dominant with sex-limited expression
mcqs.explanation: HPRT1 is on Xq26. Affected hemizygous males inherit the variant from carrier mothers. The pattern of an affected uncle on the maternal side + an affected nephew is classic X-linked recessive.
